q3: The model kept landing on data or abstraction at the end of passages — it had a habit of closing on the statistic rather than the person, which is exactly backwards from how I think about structure. I had to push on the sequencing and the closing beat more than once before it clicked.
q5: I looked at it, and honestly it captured more than I expected — 'tethering' as a concept for keeping contextual passages anchored to the central character is close to how I actually think about rhythm. Where it fell a little short is that it didn't quite articulate the sequencing instinct I care about: not just that you return to the person, but that the move back has to feel earned, not mechanical. The rubric named the destination without fully describing the road.
q6: Seeing the rubric made me notice that the model was pretty good at the structural moves in isolation — it understood tethering, it understood ending on specificity — but it kept getting the order wrong within a passage, which the rubric didn't explicitly address. The rubric treated rhythm as a section-level concern when my real frustration was sentence-level sequencing inside a single paragraph.

q3: The main recurring issue was transitions — the model kept writing hinge lines that tried to do too much, reaching for a kind of cinematic sweep that ended up feeling unearned. 'Across the country, on Saturday nights just like this one' is exactly the kind of floating abstraction I'd cut in any draft. It took a few rounds to dial that in.
q5: The rubric got a lot right — 'Scene-Level Immediacy' and 'Structural Rhythm and Tethering' are basically how I think about the zoom-in/zoom-out problem, and the rubber band metaphor is actually pretty good. What it missed is the specificity of my thing about quotes: it's not just that sources should speak early and often, it's that a quote has to do something paraphrase can't. The rubric gestures at that but doesn't quite nail it.
q6: Seeing the rubric made me realize the model was actually pretty good at the opening scene stuff from the jump — it nailed Deja's room on the first try, which I don't think I would have credited it for without having the 'Scene-Level Immediacy' criterion to compare against. What the rubric surfaced as a gap was the transition problem: the model consistently handled the zoom-in well but struggled at the seam where you move outward, which tracks with what I was pushing back on in the session.

q3: The closing movement kept tripping the model up — it wanted to give the quote some cushion, some framing, when the whole point was to let it land cold. It took a lot of rounds to get it to trust the silence after the quote instead of filling it.
q5: The rubric captured most of what I care about, especially the stuff about structural rhythm and quotes doing work the prose can't. 'Source Voice Primacy' and the idea that a quote should extend rather than confirm — that's exactly right. What it didn't quite nail is the attribution thing; 'Clean Attribution' was there but felt like a footnote when for me it's almost as important as the quote itself.
q6: Seeing the rubric made me realize the model was actually pretty good at the zoom-in/zoom-out rhythm when it had explicit criteria for it — better than I would have guessed from the earlier rounds. What it surfaced is that the model's real weak spot isn't structure, it's knowing when to stop: it consistently wanted to add one more sentence after the natural endpoint, and the rubric didn't quite address that impulse to over-close.
